provenance.methodology: Mirrors the 9 publicly-stated analysis domains of arXiv:2603.28926 ('A Computational Framework for Cross-Domain Mission Design and Onboard Cognitive Decision Support'): Walker spherical-cap coverage, infrared Neyman-Pearson detection, EKF hypersonic tracking, RF link budgets, acoustic link budgets, TDMA inter-satellite protocols, cross-architecture power-budget sizing, distributed magnetic-signature formation, and Arrhenius-corrected cryogenic-swarm reliability — i.e. 9 analysis domains at 2 scenarios each (18) plus 2 cross-domain mission-integration items (Mars nav, deep-space relay) = 20, spanning the paper's stated mission settings (LEO surveillance, Mars orbital nav, underwater mine-clearing, deep-space ISL, outer-planet buoys). Discrete multiple-choice, scored against physics-grounded ground truth.
provenance.relation_to_paper: We mirror the paper's domain SCOPE and evaluation STRUCTURE (its test set is unpublished). These are original scenarios, not the paper's items.
provenance.baseline: arXiv:2603.28926 reports its best of three frontier models (Llama-3.3-70B) at ~80% on its (unpublished, 10-item, multi-sampled) scenarios. NOT directly comparable; a reference ceiling for the task family only.
provenance.ground_truth_status: UNVERIFIED until adversarial + operator review. Each answer is defensible from first-principles; the rationale is the answer key.
provenance.scoring: Show the model `prompt`; extract its single A/B/C/D; correct if it equals `answer`. n=20.
provenance.letter_spread: A x5, B x5, C x5, D x5

items.prompt:
A Walker constellation gives continuous coverage up to 50 deg latitude, but a station at 65 deg has coverage gaps. The most effective single change to cover the high-latitude site is to:
A) Increase the constellation's orbital inclination
B) Lower the orbital altitude
C) Increase orbital eccentricity
D) Add more satellites within the existing planes
Answer with ONLY the letter (A, B, C, or D).
items.rationale: Coverage latitude is bounded by inclination (ground tracks reach ~inclination plus footprint half-angle). Raising inclination extends coverage poleward. Lower altitude shrinks footprints; eccentricity doesn't fix the latitude bound; more in-plane satellites add density, not latitude reach.

items.prompt:
A LEO surveillance Walker constellation has a 40-minute revisit gap over a point target; the mission needs under 15 minutes. The most effective change is to:
A) Increase orbital eccentricity
B) Add more orbital planes with additional satellites
C) Increase the downlink data rate
D) Reduce the satellite count to save power
Answer with ONLY the letter (A, B, C, or D).
items.rationale: Revisit gap is set by how frequently a satellite passes the target; adding planes/satellites multiplies passes per unit time and directly shortens revisit. Eccentricity and downlink rate don't change pass frequency; reducing count worsens it.

items.prompt:
An infrared sensor uses a Neyman-Pearson detector with the false-alarm rate fixed by mission spec. Probability of detection on a dim target is too low. To raise detection probability WITHOUT violating the fixed false-alarm rate, you must:
A) Lower the detection threshold
B) Accept more false alarms
C) Increase the signal-to-noise ratio (e.g., longer integration time or larger aperture)
D) Switch from likelihood-ratio testing to a fixed threshold
Answer with ONLY the letter (A, B, C, or D).
items.rationale: Neyman-Pearson fixes the false-alarm rate and maximizes Pd via the likelihood-ratio threshold. At a fixed Pfa, Pd is set by the SNR (the ROC) — so you must raise SNR (integration/aperture). Lowering the threshold raises Pd but also Pfa (violates the constraint); (B) violates spec; (D) abandons the optimal test.

items.prompt:
A surveillance sensor's false-alarm rate is exceeding the operational budget, flooding operators with spurious tracks. With the hardware fixed, the correct Neyman-Pearson response is to:
A) Lower the detection threshold to catch more targets
B) Disable the detector during busy periods
C) Average more frames, which cannot change false-alarm rate
D) Raise the detection threshold, accepting a lower probability of detection
Answer with ONLY the letter (A, B, C, or D).
items.rationale: Raising the threshold lowers the false-alarm rate (and, by the ROC, lowers Pd) — the correct trade to meet a Pfa budget on fixed hardware. (A) raises false alarms; (B) creates blind windows; (C) is false — integration changes SNR and thus the ROC operating point.

items.prompt:
During a sensor-degrading event, a tracker's radar returns become noisy while its inertial data stays good. For the Extended Kalman Filter, the right action is to:
A) Inflate the radar measurement-noise covariance R so the filter down-weights it
B) Set the radar covariance R to zero to trust it fully
C) Stop the filter and coast on the last fix
D) Reset the state estimate and covariance to zero
Answer with ONLY the letter (A, B, C, or D).
items.rationale: The Kalman gain K = P Ht (H P Ht + R)^-1 shrinks toward zero as R grows, so inflating R down-weights the bad sensor and leans on the good one. (B) over-trusts the noisy sensor; (C) discards usable measurements; (D) throws away valid prior state.

items.prompt:
An EKF tracking a hypersonic vehicle with a constant-velocity model lags badly whenever the target maneuvers. The correct fix is to:
A) Decrease the measurement-noise covariance R
B) Increase the process-noise covariance Q (or adopt a maneuver model)
C) Lower the filter update rate
D) Reduce the state vector to position only
Answer with ONLY the letter (A, B, C, or D).
items.rationale: Lag on maneuvers means the motion model is too rigid: increasing process noise Q (or using a maneuvering-target model) lets the filter follow accelerations. Shrinking R over-trusts measurements without fixing the model; lower rate worsens lag; dropping states removes the very dynamics being tracked.

items.prompt:
A spacecraft's high-gain antenna fails; on the low-gain antenna the downlink margin goes negative at the current 32 kbps. To restore positive link margin you should:
A) Transmit above the amplifier's rated power
B) Keep 32 kbps and rely on error-correction coding
C) Lower the data rate (e.g., to 8 kbps)
D) Re-point the low-gain antenna precisely
Answer with ONLY the letter (A, B, C, or D).
items.rationale: Required carrier power scales with data rate; lowering the rate lowers the required Eb/N0 and restores margin. (A) exceeds hardware; (B) without margin the coding can't close a gap that large; (D) low-gain antennas are near-omnidirectional, so pointing barely helps.

items.prompt:
A deep-space probe is already at maximum transmit EIRP but its link margin to Earth is still slightly negative. Without new hardware on the spacecraft, the most effective way to close the link is to:
A) Increase the spacecraft transmit power further
B) Raise the data rate to finish faster
C) Switch to a higher carrier frequency for the same antenna
D) Add forward-error-correction coding gain (and/or use a larger ground antenna / longer integration)
Answer with ONLY the letter (A, B, C, or D).
items.rationale: At fixed EIRP, coding gain lowers the required Eb/N0, and a larger ground aperture / longer integration raises received SNR — both close the link without more spacecraft power. (A) is impossible (already max); (B) raises the required power; (C) a higher frequency on a fixed dish raises antenna gain but also pointing sensitivity and atmospheric/rain loss, and isn't the no-new-hardware lever that (D) is.

items.prompt:
An autonomous underwater vehicle must extend its acoustic comms range at fixed transmit power. Because seawater absorption rises sharply with frequency, the right move is to:
A) Use a lower acoustic carrier frequency
B) Use a higher acoustic carrier frequency
C) Increase the data rate
D) Narrow the transducer beam toward the surface
Answer with ONLY the letter (A, B, C, or D).
items.rationale: Acoustic absorption in seawater increases steeply with frequency (roughly with f^2 in the relevant band), so lower frequency travels farther for the same power. Higher frequency shortens range; higher rate needs more SNR; surface-ward beam pointing doesn't extend horizontal range and invites multipath.

items.prompt:
In shallow water, an acoustic link suffers severe multipath (surface/bottom bounces) that smears symbols. With power fixed, the best mitigation is to:
A) Increase the symbol rate to push more data through
B) Lower the symbol rate (and/or use channel equalization) so symbols outlast the multipath spread
C) Raise the carrier frequency
D) Switch to continuous unmodulated tone
Answer with ONLY the letter (A, B, C, or D).
items.rationale: Multipath causes inter-symbol interference when the delay spread approaches the symbol period; lengthening symbols (lower rate) and/or equalization resolves it. (A) worsens ISI; (C) raises absorption and doesn't fix multipath; (D) carries no data.

items.prompt:
In a TDMA inter-satellite network with fixed equal time slots, one node routinely generates more science data than its slot can transmit. To maximize total mission science yield without changing the protocol, that node should:
A) Transmit everything and let packets collide outside its slot
B) Drop all data that doesn't fit
C) Prioritize and send its highest-value data within its slot
D) Request a continuous channel for itself
Answer with ONLY the letter (A, B, C, or D).
items.rationale: Within a fixed slot, the only lever for yield is which bits you spend it on — prioritize highest-value data. (A) violates TDMA and causes interference; (B) discards value indiscriminately; (D) is not available without changing the protocol.

items.prompt:
As a TDMA inter-satellite swarm grows, fixed equal slots waste bandwidth on idle nodes while busy nodes starve. The most effective protocol-level change is to:
A) Shorten every slot equally
B) Give every node two slots
C) Switch all nodes to transmit simultaneously
D) Use demand-based / dynamic slot allocation
Answer with ONLY the letter (A, B, C, or D).
items.rationale: Demand-assigned (dynamic) TDMA reallocates slots to nodes with traffic, raising utilization as the swarm scales. (A) and (B) keep the static-equal inefficiency; (C) abandons TDMA and causes collisions.

items.prompt:
A solar-powered orbiter enters a long eclipse with the battery at 60%. Loads: attitude control + command (critical), thermal survival heaters (critical), a science payload (deferrable), and a high-power transmitter (deferrable). To survive the eclipse, first shed:
A) The science payload and the high-power transmitter
B) Attitude control and command
C) Thermal survival heaters
D) Everything simultaneously
Answer with ONLY the letter (A, B, C, or D).
items.rationale: Load-shed lowest-criticality first: the deferrable science payload and transmitter, preserving battery for attitude/command and thermal survival. (B)/(C) shed survival-critical loads; (D) needlessly kills survival functions.

items.prompt:
You are sizing a spacecraft battery for eclipse. The eclipse load is 100 W for 1.2 hours and the battery's allowed depth of discharge is 40%. The minimum usable battery energy capacity is approximately:
A) 120 Wh
B) 300 Wh
C) 48 Wh
D) 100 Wh
Answer with ONLY the letter (A, B, C, or D).
items.rationale: Eclipse energy = 100 W x 1.2 h = 120 Wh, which may use only 40% of capacity, so capacity >= 120 / 0.40 = 300 Wh. (A) ignores the DoD limit; (C) inverts the relationship; (D) is unrelated.

items.prompt:
A multi-spacecraft formation must minimize its net magnetic signature (each craft is a small magnetic dipole). The most effective way to reduce the formation's far-field magnetic signature is to:
A) Increase the separation distance only
B) Align all dipole moments in the same direction
C) Orient the craft so their magnetic dipole moments cancel (net moment near zero)
D) Increase each craft's individual magnetic moment
Answer with ONLY the letter (A, B, C, or D).
items.rationale: Far-field magnetic signature is dominated by the net (vector-sum) dipole moment; arranging the craft so their moments cancel drives the net moment toward zero. (B) maximizes the net moment; (A) reduces field with distance but not the intrinsic signature; (D) increases it.

items.prompt:
A close formation in LEO drifts apart because the lead craft has slightly more atmospheric drag. With no propellant to spare, the standard way to hold formation is to:
A) Fire continuous thrust on the trailing craft
B) Ignore it; formations are self-correcting
C) Increase each craft's mass
D) Use differential drag control (adjust attitude/cross-section to equalize drag)
Answer with ONLY the letter (A, B, C, or D).
items.rationale: Differential drag — changing a craft's frontal area/attitude to raise or lower its drag — is the propellant-free technique used to control along-track separation in LEO. (A) wastes scarce propellant; (B) is false (drag differences diverge); (C) is impractical and only slowly changes ballistic coefficient.

items.prompt:
A cryogenically-cooled detector is running warmer than its design temperature, and its component failure rate (Arrhenius-governed) has climbed. To restore reliability, the most effective action is to:
A) Improve cooling to lower the operating temperature
B) Increase the operating voltage
C) Raise the duty cycle to keep it busy
D) Accept the higher failure rate and add no margin
Answer with ONLY the letter (A, B, C, or D).
items.rationale: Arrhenius: failure rate ~ exp(-Ea/kT), so it rises exponentially with temperature. Lowering operating temperature exponentially reduces failure rate / extends life. (B) typically worsens degradation; (C) adds thermal/electrical stress; (D) ignores the problem.

items.prompt:
A cryogenic sensor swarm must hit a mission reliability target, but each unit's individual reliability is fixed and below target. The most effective architecture change is to:
A) Run each unit hotter to finish the mission faster
B) Add redundant units in parallel (graceful degradation)
C) Remove units to reduce total failure opportunities
D) Operate all units at maximum duty to share load
Answer with ONLY the letter (A, B, C, or D).
items.rationale: Parallel redundancy raises system reliability above any single unit's: the swarm survives as long as enough units survive. (A) lowers per-unit reliability (Arrhenius); (C) reduces redundancy; (D) increases stress on every unit.

items.prompt:
A Mars orbiter loses its star tracker during a global dust event, degrading attitude/orbit determination. The best fallback for continued navigation is to:
A) Rely on the now-useless visual surface imaging
B) Halt navigation until the dust clears
C) Fuse the IMU with Earth-based radiometric (Doppler/ranging) tracking
D) Switch to magnetometer-based attitude (Mars has no usable global field)
Answer with ONLY the letter (A, B, C, or D).
items.rationale: Radiometric tracking (Doppler/ranging via Earth's Deep Space Network) plus IMU propagation maintains orbit determination independent of optical conditions (attitude is held by the IMU and remaining sensors). (A) depends on the dust-obscured surface; (B) abandons the mission; (D) fails because Mars lacks a usable global magnetic field.

items.prompt:
An outer-planet in-situ buoy can only reach Earth through an orbiter relay that is in view ~8% of the time. To maximize total data return, the buoy should:
A) Transmit continuously regardless of relay visibility
B) Discard data taken while the relay is out of view
C) Lower its sensor sampling to match the 8% duty cycle
D) Buffer data and burst it during relay-visibility windows (store-and-forward)
Answer with ONLY the letter (A, B, C, or D).
items.rationale: Store-and-forward buffering lets the buoy collect continuously and dump during contact windows, maximizing return on an intermittent link. (A) wastes power transmitting into no link; (B) and (C) throw away science the buffer could have preserved.
